title: Hardware Performance: FLOPS
concept: FLOPS (FLOPs per second) measures hardware speed; GPU cluster efficiency is graded by how close it gets to peak theoretical FLOPS (MFU).

exercises.prompt: When we measure a computer's speed in FLOPS, what are we actually counting?

options.text: The weight of the graphics card
The number of math calculations it can do in one second
How many fans are spinning inside the computer

exercises.explain: FLOPS stands for Floating-Point Operations Per Second and is the standard metric used to measure calculations per second.

cards.title: Measuring Computer Muscle
cards.body: Have you ever wondered how we compare the raw muscle power of different AI supercomputers? We don't measure it by how pretty the computer case is, but by counting how many math calculations it can crush in a single second!

cards.title: What is FLOPS?
cards.body: FLOPS stands for Floating-Point Operations Per Second. It measures how many math calculations (like adding or multiplying decimals) a computer chip can complete in one single second. The higher the FLOPS, the faster the hardware runs!

cards.title: The Master Chef speed
cards.body: Imagine you hire a master chef who claims they can chop 100 carrots per minute (their Peak FLOPS). In a real kitchen, they also have to wash hands, open drawers, and clean up, so they only chop 40 carrots per minute. Their real-world speed is their actual FLOPS, and their efficiency score (MFU) is 40%!

cards.title: Real-World Performance
cards.body: A modern AI chip might have a peak performance of 300 TeraFLOPS (300 trillion calculations per second). But when training a real AI chatbot, the chip might only achieve 120 TeraFLOPS because it spends time waiting for data. This gives it a Model FLOPs Utilization (MFU) of 40%.

cards.title: The Peak Speed Illusion
cards.body: People often think that buying a chip rated at '100 TeraFLOPS' means their AI programs will run at exactly that speed. In reality, actual speeds are much lower because computers waste time moving data around. Always look at the actual efficiency (MFU)!

cards.title: Peak vs. Actual Speed
cards.body: Peak FLOPS is what the manufacturer advertises on the box under perfect conditions. Actual FLOPS is what you actually get when running your AI program.

left.text: Theoretical max speed on an empty test track with no wind resistance.

right.text: Real-world speed while navigating heavy traffic and red lights.

exercises.prompt: What does the acronym FLOPS stand for in hardware performance?

options.text: File Loading and Operating Process Speed

exercises.explain: FLOPS stands for Floating-Point Operations Per Second. It is the universal yardstick for comparing the computational speed of hardware.

exercises.prompt: True or False: A higher Model FLOPs Utilization (MFU) means your AI cluster is running more efficiently.
exercises.explain: MFU (Model FLOPs Utilization) measures efficiency. A higher MFU means the system is waste-free and running closer to its maximum hardware capability.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the explanation of hardware speed.
exercises.sentence: We use [BLANK] to measure the raw number of math calculations a chip performs each second.
exercises.explain: FLOPS measures the speed of hardware by counting calculations per second, while MFU is the percentage score of how close we get to that limit.

exercises.prompt: Which statement accurately describes real-world AI hardware performance?

options.text: Real-world FLOPS are usually much lower than peak theoretical FLOPS.
Hardware always runs at exactly 100% of its peak theoretical FLOPS.
Real-world training performance is faster than peak theoretical FLOPS.

exercises.explain: The first statement is correct. Peak FLOPS is a theoretical laboratory maximum, whereas real-world workloads always run slower due to data transfer bottlenecks.

exercises.prompt: Why does a massive AI GPU cluster never actually reach 100% of its peak theoretical FLOPS in real-world training?

options.text: Real-world workloads run much faster than the peak speed of the chips
Real GPUs instantly receive data with zero delay or transfer time
Data transfer delays and coordination overhead prevent GPUs from reaching peak speeds
Peak theoretical FLOPS represent the minimum possible speed of the hardware

exercises.explain: Theoretical peak FLOPS assume absolute perfection under laboratory conditions. In the real world, chips waste time waiting for data to travel through wires, cooling down, and communicating with each other, meaning the actual measured FLOPS are always lower.
